$comment: Routing + lifecycle index for the frontier research layer. Audited by tools/_audit_research.py (run from research/): every entry's path must exist on disk, and every on-disk note/experiment/report/banked collection must have an entry here. Trust model: research claims are sourced + tiered + dated and they STALE; cite as research/notes/<file>.md, EXP-NN, or RR-NN — never as a Book NN §X. Banked collections are frozen generation data with NO citation authority (each collection's README carries its trust model).
staleness_policy: A note stales ~180 days after its 'reviewed:' date. Re-verify load-bearing claims (and bump the date) before planning against it. The audit warns on stale notes; --strict-staleness makes staleness a failure.

notes.summary: Web-sourced, tiered version specifics for Angular 22 / Material 22 / CDK 22 / Angular Aria: release date, Node/TS/RxJS floors, default application builder, zoneless+OnPush defaults and the stable provideZonelessChangeDetection() API, the stable signal/resource/Signal-Forms APIs, CVA-still-the-bridge, and the MIT+Google provenance of the core stack. Grounds Book 01 and the M0 scaffold/provenance slices.

Web-sourced, tiered version/provenance specifics for TanStack Table behind Caelum's data-grid adapter (D-07): @tanstack/table-core 8.21.3 (MIT, zero deps) + @tanstack/angular-table 8.21.4 (MIT; deps tslib + exact-pinned table-core; peer @angular/core >=17, signal-based FlexRender); headless = renders no DOM (you own the markup); core API surface (ColumnDef, table instance, opt-in row models); and the clean two-package permissive US-origin transitive tree (tslib 0BSD/Microsoft) — with origin/transitive sign-off explicitly deferred to M2. Grounds Book 13.

Web-sourced, tiered grounding for the charts gap (D-08), with one decisive finding: visx (Airbnb, MIT) is REACT-BOUND (hard react ^18||^19 peer on @visx/shape), so it is structurally unavailable to an Angular library — the render-it-yourself approach D-08 endorsed runs on D3's framework-agnostic modules directly (d3-scale 4.0.2 ISC + d3-shape 3.2.0 ISC, Mike Bostock/US, no react peer; scales + arc/line/area path generators). D3 emits only numbers and path strings (no DOM, no CSS) so the cae-chart draws SVG with --cae-* tokens itself — the ideal token-bridge fit. D-08 needs a REFINEMENT not a reversal (visx approach right, visx package wrong for Angular → use D3 directly); filed for the M2 sign-off; charts deferred for the build. d3 transitive tree (bushier than the grid's) + origin sign-off defer to M2. Grounds Book 14.

Web-sourced, tiered grounding for the editor gap (D-09), with one decisive finding that is the FAVORABLE MIRROR of the visx result: Lexical's core engine is framework-agnostic — core `lexical` 0.46.0 (MIT, facebook/lexical) declares NO react/react-dom dep or peer (sole dep @lexical/internal, a zero-dep MIT leaf), and the headless behavior modules (@lexical/rich-text, @lexical/history, @lexical/utils) are React-free too; the React peer is quarantined entirely in the separately-published @lexical/react binding Angular never imports. So unlike charts, D-09 stands as-is (no refinement): Lexical is consumable in Angular as a real adapter via createEditor()/setRootElement() onto a cae-editor contenteditable + registerRichText/registerHistory, all in one editor.adapter.ts membrane. Cleanest provenance of the three gaps (single-origin Meta MIT tree over a zero-dep leaf); the contenteditable fallback is a genuine floor for a small need; zoneless bridging (listener→signal) needs care. Editor deferred for the build (cut order #1); transitive sign-off defers to M2. Grounds Book 15.

Web-sourced, tiered grounding for the a11y/parity-verification TOOLING of Book 16 (Volume IV), with one load-bearing provenance finding: axe-core 4.12.1 is MPL-2.0 (a weak/file-level COPYLEFT license, NOT permissive like MIT) maintained by Deque (US, Virginia), zero runtime/peer deps — and it is admissible PRECISELY because it is a DEV/TEST tool never shipped in Caelum's distributed package, so MPL's copyleft doesn't reach Caelum's source (Book 03 §3.3's runtime-vs-dev distinction made concrete — a license rejected for a runtime dep is fine for a CI tool). @axe-core/playwright 4.12.1 (MPL-2.0; dep axe-core, peer playwright-core >=1.0.0) drives axe in a real browser. The Angular CDK component test harness (TestbedHarnessEnvironment for unit, SeleniumWebDriverHarnessEnvironment for e2e; documentRootLoader for overlays) is confirmed current in the v22 docs as the parity-scenario mechanism. The capability ledger (untouched->implementer-passed->adversarial-passed) + pre-committed parity scenarios are CAELUM artifacts from brief §6/§7, not tool features; axe is necessary-not-sufficient (keyboard + SR + the adversarial pass do the rest). Caveats: WCAG 2.2 tag coverage + the ~30-40% automated-catch figure NOT re-sourced (flagged, not asserted); the test-RUNNER choice is Book 17's, not this note's. Grounds Book 16; dev-tier provenance sign-off at M4/CI.

Web-sourced, tiered grounding for Book 17 (Testing Strategy & Tooling), settling the frontier question Book 16 deferred: Angular 22's default unit-test runner. Directly fetched from angular.dev (served as v22.0.4): Vitest is the DEFAULT runner for new projects, wired via the @angular/build:unit-test builder (tsConfig tsconfig.spec.json, buildTarget ::development); Karma is still SUPPORTED as a legacy alternative (not removed in v22 — correcting the common 'Karma is dead' framing). Migrating an EXISTING Karma project is explicitly experimental (the refactor-jasmine-vitest schematic is experimental and partial) — but greenfield Caelum is BORN on Vitest, the stable path, so it never walks the migration path. Default test env is Node + jsdom (happy-dom swappable); Vitest Browser Mode runs real-browser tests via a Playwright/WebdriverIO provider (opt-in) — relevant to layout/zoneless. Provenance (all DEV-TIER, never shipped): Vitest 4.1.9 MIT (antfu/VoidZero), @playwright/test 1.61.1 Apache-2.0/Microsoft/US; the whole runner+harness+visual toolchain is governed by Book 03 §3.3's runtime-vs-dev split (free+unshipped), not the runtime US-origin bar (M0-4 gate must exclude devDependencies from the shipped-tree scan). The CDK ComponentHarness (runner-agnostic, the highest-leverage test artifact) is cross-referenced from a11y-testing-tooling.md, not re-fetched. Caveats: jsdom/browser-mode specifics are WebSearch-derived (verify flag names against angular.dev/guide/testing/components); library (ng-packagr) vs application test wiring is an OPEN question to resolve at M0. Grounds Book 17.

Web-sourced, tiered grounding for Book 18 (Performance & Bundle Budgets — completes Volume IV), gathered via a 6-facet research fan-out plus first-hand re-fetches of the load-bearing version/license claims. Directly fetched from angular.dev (v22.0.4): (1) BUNDLE BUDGETS in angular.json — six types (bundle/initial/allScript/all/anyComponentStyle/anyScript), thresholds (baseline/maximumWarning/maximumError/minimumWarning/minimumError/warning/error, bytes/kb/mb/% units), the GATE (warning=build succeeds, error=ng build FAILS), type-level defaults (initial 500kb/1mb, anyComponentStyle 2kb/4kb). (2) TREE-SHAKING / Angular Package Format (the Caelum-central lever) — FESM2022, 'sideEffects': false strongly recommended, partial compilation (consumer compiles the lib), SECONDARY ENTRY POINTS via exports for fine-grained code splitting (Material ships each component separately), ng-packagr AOT, tree-shakable provide*()/providedIn services. (3) @defer deferrable views — split deferred STANDALONE deps into separate chunks, triggers (idle/viewport/interaction/hover/immediate/timer/when + prefetch), @placeholder/@loading/@error. (4) PROFILING — Angular DevTools Profiler (bar chart of CD cycles, flame graph, the 'Change detection' checkbox greys OnPush-skipped components, JSON export) + Chrome DevTools ng.enableProfiling() (dev-mode). (5) DEV-TIER analysis tooling — @angular/build 22.0.4 MIT (esbuild 0.28.1/Vite 7.3.5/Rollup 4.60.2), ng build --stats-json → esbuild analyzer (first-party, zero-dep), source-map-explorer 2.5.3 Apache-2.0 (builder-agnostic), webpack-bundle-analyzer webpack-only (fit mismatch); all dev-tier (Book 03 §3.3). Zoneless/OnPush CD facts cross-referenced from angular-22-platform.md. KEY STRUCTURAL INSIGHT: angular.json budgets gate the APPLICATION build (Forge), NOT the ng-packagr LIBRARY build — so Caelum's shipped-package size gate is a SEPARATE CI mechanism (size-limit/source-map-explorer per secondary entry point) to wire at M0-4. Caveats flag honestly: no memorized 'zoneless saves N KB' (measure it); virtual-scroll constants + ng-new generated budgets read from main branch (verify at tag); @defer stability stated behaviorally; SSR/incremental-hydration + router-preloading + Web-Vitals runtime gates under-covered. Grounds Book 18.

Web-sourced, tiered grounding for Book 19 (Packaging, Versioning & Distribution — OPENS Volume V), gathered via a 5-facet research fan-out fetched this session from angular.dev (v22.0.4 footers), the ng-packagr repo/schema, the angular-cli spec, docs.npmjs.com, and slsa.dev. (1) LIBRARY BUILD — the v22 library builder is @angular/build:ng-packagr (always AOT), distinct from the esbuild application builder; partial compilation ('compilationMode':'partial') is mandatory; ng-package.json config (dest/lib.entryFile/...); ng-packagr 22.0.0 MIT released 2026-06-03 (Angular GA day), main on 22.1.0-next.2, a DEV/build-tier tool; publish flow is ng build --configuration production -> cd dist/<lib> -> npm publish. (2) CONSUMER CONTRACT — APF emits 'type':'module' + a conditional 'exports' map keyed ONLY by 'types' + 'default' per entry point (CORRECTION: the widely-blogged 'esm2022' condition is NOT in the v22 spec), fesm2022/*.mjs, bundled types/*.d.ts, 'sideEffects':false; SECONDARY ENTRY POINTS are separate top-level exports keys (independently importable + tree-shakable, Material ships each component this way), discovered by ng-packagr via an ng-package.json (can be '{}') in a subdirectory; a 'sass' condition exposes theme sub-paths. (3) DEPENDENCY DECLARATION — @angular/* as peerDependencies NOT dependencies (else duplicate-Angular-instance breakage); angular.dev prescribes NO range syntax in prose — the observed convention from Angular's own CDK/Material (22.0.2) is the multi-major caret-OR '^22.0.0 || ^23.0.0' (Material pins @angular/cdk exactly = one unit); ship an 'ng-update' block (migrations/packageGroup/requirements; per-migration version+factory) so consumers auto-migrate. (4) VERSIONING — Angular semver (breaking only in major), major every 6 months, 1-3 minors/major, 18-month support (6 active + 12 LTS), deprecation survives >=2 majors (>=12 months), peer-dep carve-out (minors widen ranges, require only at next major), Developer-Preview/experimental APIs exempt (can break in a patch). (5) DISTRIBUTION — npm publish --provenance / Trusted Publishing from GitHub Actions (OIDC id-token:write, ubuntu-latest; auto-provenance for public repo+package, no flag needed) attests source repo/commit/workflow/CI/digest, signed by Sigstore (Fulcio cert + Rekor log) = SLSA Build L2 + npm badge, verify via npm audit signatures. KEYSTONE: npm/SLSA provenance attests BUILD origin (which repo/commit/CI produced the tarball) — ORTHOGONAL to Caelum's US-ORIGIN claim (who authored/owns the code + transitive tree); provenance is necessary-but-not-sufficient, so the US-origin attestation (Book 03 §3.3) ships as a SEPARATE artifact in the package. Caveats: 'esm2022' not a v22 key; peer-range is convention not doc-guidance; ng-update schema is from the (undated) angular-cli spec repo not a v22-tagged page; manual --provenance on private packages unconfirmed; SLSA predicate version emitted today unconfirmed; Material-per-component package.json not re-fetched (403). Grounds Book 19; feasibility path + 3 candidate experiments (packaging-shape / provenance / ng-update) recorded.
